entries.title: Overview of Burp settings - user vs project, search, import/export
entries.body:
# Burp Suite settings - the big picture

All of Burp's parameters are gathered in a single **Settings** dialog. It opens from the top menu: the **Settings** item on the main window panel. Inside, the settings are grouped into sections - **Tools**, **Project**, **Sessions**, **Network**, **User interface**, **Suite**, **AI**, **Extensions**, **Configuration library** - and on the left there is a navigation tree for these groups.

## The main distinction: user settings versus project settings

This is the key idea, without which Burp's settings are constantly confused. The parameters are split into two storage levels:

* **User settings** - apply to **your entire Burp installation** on this machine: they apply to all disk projects and to temporary projects. They are stored separately from the project, in the user profile. This is the logical place for everything that should not change from project to project: hotkeys, theme, fonts, the behavior of Burp's browser.
* **Project settings** - apply **only to the current project** and are saved **inside the project file**. This is what is specific to a particular test: the target scope, the project's Collaborator settings, tasks, logging, automatic backup.

### Override options for this project only

Some settings can exist at both levels. They have an **Override options for this project only** toggle: by default the user value is used, but by enabling the override you set your own value for **this** project, without touching the global default. The classic example is the Collaborator settings: there is one global default, but in a particular project you can specify a private server.

## Searching the settings

There are many settings, so at the top of the dialog there is a **search bar** - you type part of a parameter name, and the list filters on the fly. Next to it are the **All / User / Project** filter buttons: quickly narrow the output to only user or only project settings. This is the fastest way to find the parameter you need without remembering which section it is in.

## Saving, loading and resetting

Burp's configuration can be exported to an **external JSON file** and carried between projects/machines:

* **Save settings** - export the current configuration (the selected set) to JSON.
* **Load settings** - import a configuration from a JSON file.
* **Restore default settings** - return the parameters to factory values.

Working with whole sets is convenient through **Manage global settings** - from there both user and project settings are exported/imported in full. Individual parameters have their own action icons for saving/loading specifically those.

> Connection with the Configuration library: saved JSON configs are exactly the building blocks that can later be attached to scan tasks.

## Practice and safety

* **Set up your own base JSON profile.** Configure hotkeys, theme, fonts, Repeater/Intruder behavior once, export to JSON, and load it on every new machine - without configuring everything from scratch.
* **Remember where a setting lives.** If a parameter "does not carry over" to a new project - it is a project setting and sits in the project file; if, conversely, it "follows" into all projects - it is a user setting.
* **Import only trusted files.** The documentation warns directly: load project files and configs only from trusted sources - someone else's project file may contain settings that affect your environment.

```
Where to look for what:
- hotkeys, theme, fonts, Burp's browser -> usually User settings
- scope, Collaborator (project), tasks, logging, automatic backup -> Project settings
- don't remember the section -> the search bar at the top + the All/User/Project filter
```

entries.title: Key settings - the parameters you configure first
entries.body:
# Key settings

This page is not a separate tab but a navigator collection: PortSwigger has gathered here the parameters that are most often needed at the start of work and have the strongest effect on the behavior of all of Burp. Treat it as a checklist of "what to configure before you start testing."

## 1. Target scope

**Target scope** tells Burp which hosts and URLs are currently of interest to you. This is a fundamental setting: the filtering in the **site map**, in the **Proxy history** and the operation of the **Scanner** are all tied to scope. It is worth setting the scope correctly **at the very beginning** - then Burp's tools will not touch extraneous targets, and you will not drown in someone else's traffic.

> Practice: configure the scope first thing, otherwise you risk attacking something other than what the bug bounty program allows.

## 2. Platform authentication

If the application/server itself requires a login at the HTTP level, Burp can automatically supply credentials. Three schemes are supported: **Basic**, **NTLMv1** and **NTLMv2**. You can set **different credentials for different hosts** - convenient when a single test involves several protected systems.

## 3. Session handling rules and macros - getting around testing obstacles

Many defense mechanisms interfere with automated testing: the server drops the session on suspicious activity, requires one-time tokens in every request (anti-CSRF), ties actions to multi-step processes. **Session handling rules** and **macros** allow Burp to automatically replay these steps (log in again, fetch a fresh token) and continue the test without manual intervention.

## 4. Task scheduler

**Task scheduler** lets you configure the automatic launch of certain tasks at a given time. Typical uses: run heavy operations at night/during off-hours and periodically save work progress.

## 5. HTTP message display

This is where you configure how Burp **displays HTTP messages**: the font and character set in the request/response editors, as well as the font of Burp's interface itself. A minor thing, but over long sessions the right monospace font and correct encoding save your eyes and help you not lose characters in non-standard data.

## Summary - the order of actions at the start

```
1. Target scope     -> limit the targets (site map / Proxy / Scanner)
2. Platform auth    -> supply Basic/NTLM credentials per host, if needed
3. Session handling -> macros/rules against session drops and tokens
4. Task scheduler   -> heavy tasks at night, periodic saving
5. Message display  -> a convenient font/encoding for the editors and UI
```

entries.title: Tool settings - where the settings of individual tools live
entries.body:
# Tool settings (Tools)

The **Tools** section in the **Settings** dialog is the "root" from which the settings of each individual Burp tool open. The page itself does not configure anything: it is a table-of-contents pointer leading to specific subpages. It is useful to know exactly what lives where, so as not to hunt for a parameter at random.

## Which tools have their own settings

* **Proxy** - the largest section: proxy listeners and certificates, request/response and WebSocket interception rules, response modification, match and replace, TLS pass through, history logging, the startup interception state and a set of "miscellaneous" HTTP options.
* **Intruder** - the behavior of the brute-forcing tool at the UI and project level: the position of the side panel, how payload markers are placed, configuration of new tabs, what to do with results windows, the source of payload lists.
* **Repeater** - connection reuse (HTTP/1 and HTTP/2), redirect handling, updating Content-Length, decompressing compressed responses, streaming timeout, tab appearance.
* **Comparer** - literally one option: synchronizing panels in new comparison results windows.
* **Sequencer** - the settings of the token randomness analyzer (on this overview page it is also listed among the tools that have settings).
* **Burp's browser** - the built-in Chromium: storing browser data, running without a sandbox, using the GPU.

## How to use it

```
Settings -> Tools -> <tool>
```

If you do not remember which tool a parameter is in, do not open sections one by one - use the **search bar** at the top of the Settings dialog: it searches across all sections at once.

> Note: the tools' settings are mixed in terms of storage level. For example, in Burp's browser some options are user-level (storing data), some are project-level (GPU/sandbox). Look at the labels in each specific section.

entries.title: Proxy settings - listeners, interception, match and replace, TLS pass through
entries.body:
# Proxy settings

Proxy is the heart of Burp, and it has the most settings of all. We go through the page's sections from top to bottom.

## Proxy listeners

Local HTTP proxies that listen for connections from the browser. By default there is one listener: port **8080** on loopback (`127.0.0.1`).

* **Bind to port** - the port to listen on.
* **Bind to address** - the interface: loopback only, all interfaces or a specific IP. Opening on all interfaces is a deliberate choice, it lets others on the network into the proxy.
* **Redirect to host / Redirect to port** - forcibly redirect all traffic to the specified host/port.
* **Force use of TLS** - always establish outgoing connections over HTTPS.
* **Support invisible proxying** - "invisible" proxy mode for clients that cannot work through an explicit proxy (when traffic is routed to Burp at the network level).

**Listener certificates:** self-signed; **per-host certificates signed by Burp's CA** (the default option - which is why you need to install Burp's CA certificate in the browser); CA-signed with a specific hostname; or your own certificate in **PKCS#12** format. Separately you configure the set of **TLS protocols** (Java default or manual choice) and **HTTP/2** (enabled by default; turn it off for problematic clients).

## Request and response interception rules

They define which messages get to interception in **Intercept**. The main flag is **Intercept requests/responses based on the following rules**. Each rule: a boolean operator (**And/Or**), a feature type (domain, IP, protocol, method, file extension, etc.), a relation (**Matches / Does not match**) and a value (with regex support). Here too:

* **Automatically update Content-Length header when the request is edited** - recalculate the body length on manual editing.
* **Automatically fix missing or superfluous new lines at end of request** - fix excess/missing line breaks at the end of the request.

## WebSocket interception rules

* **Intercept client-to-server messages** - intercept messages from the client to the server.
* **Intercept server-to-client messages** - intercept messages from the server to the client.
* **Only intercept in-scope messages** - only in-scope messages.

## Response modification rules - modifying responses "on the fly"

A set of checkboxes that automatically rewrite incoming responses, simplifying manual analysis of the client side:

* **Unhide hidden form fields** / **Prominently highlight unhidden fields** - reveal hidden form fields and highlight them.
* **Enable disabled form fields** - enable disabled fields.
* **Remove input field length limits** - remove field length limits.
* **Remove JavaScript form validation** - remove client-side JS form validation.
* **Remove all JavaScript** / **Remove <object> tags** - strip all JS / `<object>` tags.
* **Convert HTTPS links to HTTP** / **Remove secure flag from cookies** - techniques for SSL-strip scenarios.

> These are edits **in the tester's browser**, not on the server: convenient for manually toggling hidden/disabled elements.

## HTTP and WebSocket match and replace rules

Automatic replacement of message content as it passes through the proxy, by "find -> replace" rules (for example, substituting the `User-Agent` header in all requests). It is configured here or through the **Proxy > Match and replace** tab.

## TLS pass through - transparent proxy without decryption

Hosts from this list Burp **passes through as-is, without decrypting or modifying** the TLS traffic. Why: to get around **certificate pinning** and not break third-party services (telemetry, updaters) that are of no interest anyway. Host/IP range (regex supported), port; loading the list from CSV/text; **Automatically add entries on client TLS negotiation failure** - auto-add a host if the client rejected Burp's certificate. It can be applied to out-of-scope items (**Apply to out-of-scope items**).

## Default Proxy history message display

Which message variant to show in the history by default for requests/responses/WebSockets: **Original**, **Edited**, **Auto-modified**.

## Proxy history logging - what to do with out-of-scope

Behavior for items outside the scope: stop logging them; ask each time when adding to scope; do nothing.

## Default Proxy interception state - the startup interception state

What to do with interception at startup: enable it; disable it; restore the state from the previous session. Most people keep interception **disabled** by default, so that the proxy does not "get stuck" on every request.

## Miscellaneous - other (HTTP-related)

A set of low-level flags: use **HTTP/1.0** in requests to the server / responses to the client; **keep-alive** for HTTP/1; set `Connection: close` on outgoing/incoming; **Strip Proxy-* headers**; remove unsupported encodings from `Accept-Encoding`; **Strip Sec-WebSocket-Extensions**; decompress compressed requests/responses (gzip/Deflate/Brotli); **Disable web interface at http://burpsuite**; suppress Burp errors in the browser; and do not send items to Proxy history/live tasks (optional - only if out of scope, to save memory on noisy targets).

```
A minimal reasonable Proxy start:
- listener 127.0.0.1:8080, Burp's CA certificate in the browser
- interception OFF by default (Default interception state)
- pinning breaks the connection -> add the host to TLS pass through
- do not log out-of-scope -> Proxy history logging
```

entries.title: Intruder settings - panel, payload markers, new tabs, results windows
entries.body:
# Intruder settings

It is important to separate two concepts right away. The **settings** of Intruder on this page are the behavior of the tool as a whole (how the UI behaves, what happens to results). The parameters of a **specific attack** - the number of threads, delays, retries - are set not here, but in the attack's **resource pool** and its configuration. Do not look for throttle/threads here - they are not here.

## Default Intruder side panel layout - the side panel

* **Position** - where to keep the side panel: on the left (**Left**) or on the right (**Right**).
* Expansion mode: **Auto-expand** - the panel expands and collapses by itself depending on the available screen space; or always collapsed (**Always collapsed**).

## Automatic payload placement - auto-placement of markers

When Burp places the payload positions itself (`§...§`), it can either **replace** the value of the base parameter or **append** the payload to it. The default behavior is chosen here - a minor thing that saves time on manually editing markers.

## New tab configuration - the startup configuration of new tabs

With which configuration a new attack tab opens:

* use the **default configuration**;
* **copy the configuration from the first** tab;
* **copy the configuration from the last** tab.

Convenient when you run similar attacks: you configure it once - new tabs inherit the settings.

## Behavior when closing results windows

Here Burp asks what to do when you close an attack window. Separately for **running** and **completed** attacks.

For an **attack in progress**:

* continue in the background (**Continue my attack in the background**);
* delete the attack (**Delete my attack**);
* ask each time (**Ask me what to do each time**).

For a **completed** attack:

* save to the project file (**Save my attack to the project file**);
* keep in memory (**Keep in memory**);
* delete (**Delete my attack**);
* ask each time.

> Practice: "keep in memory" risks losing the results on restart; for important brute-force runs choose saving to the project.

## Payload list location - the source of payload lists

Where to take the built-in lists: use Burp's **built-in** lists or load **your own** from a specified directory. There is a **Copy** option to copy Burp's preconfigured lists into your own folder and edit them further.

```
In short:
- threads/throttle/retries -> NOT here, but in the attack's resource pool
- here: the panel, auto payload markers, inheritance of tab configuration,
        the fate of results windows, the source of payload lists
```

entries.title: Repeater settings - connections, redirects, Content-Length, tab appearance
entries.body:
# Repeater settings

Repeater is the manual sending and re-sending of requests. Its settings control how exactly a request goes to the server and how the response is handled.

## Connections - connection reuse

* **HTTP/1 connection reuse** - reuse the TCP connection for HTTP/1 requests instead of opening a new one for each request/response pair. Sometimes the server's behavior depends on whether the connection is new or not.
* **HTTP/2 connection reuse** - reuse a single TCP connection for several HTTP/2 requests. You can turn it off if the server handles the first request in a connection differently from the rest.
* **Allow HTTP/2 ALPN override** - allows sending HTTP/2 even if the server does not announce HTTP/2 support via ALPN. Needed for a number of protocol-level attacks.

## Message modification - editing the message before sending

* **Update Content-Length** - automatically recalculate the `Content-Length` header when the request has a body. Usually kept enabled, otherwise the server will reject a request with an incorrect length.
* **Unpack compressed responses** - automatically decompress responses in gzip, deflate and Brotli, so as to see a readable body.
* **Normalize HTTP/1 line endings** - add `\r` before `\n`, so as not to send an HTTP/1 request that is invalid on line breaks.
* **Strip Connection header over HTTP/2** - remove the `Connection` header from HTTP/2 requests before sending (in HTTP/2 it is forbidden).

## Redirects - handling redirections

The **Follow redirects** mode:

* **Never** - do not follow;
* **On-site only** - only to the same domain;
* **In-scope only** - only to targets in scope;
* **Always** - always follow.

Additionally: **Process cookies in redirects** - re-send the cookie from the redirect response (important when the login flow runs through several 30x); **Use selected protocol for cross-domain redirects** - apply the protocol selected in Inspector to cross-domain redirects.

## Streaming responses timeout

How long to keep a streaming connection open. By default **600 seconds** (10 minutes). Raise/lower it for a particular server that keeps the stream open.

## Default tab group

Which tab group new requests sent to Repeater go into. Helps not to clutter a single feed when there are many tabs.

## Tab view - the appearance of the tab row

* **Scrolling view** - tabs in a single scrollable row with a dropdown search across them;
* **Wrapped view** - tabs wrap onto several rows, all visible at once.

```
Reasonable Repeater defaults:
- Update Content-Length: ON
- Unpack compressed responses: ON
- Normalize HTTP/1 line endings: ON (fewer random broken requests)
- Follow redirects: Never or In-scope only (controllable)
```

entries.title: Comparer settings - the single panel-synchronization option
entries.body:
# Comparer settings

The shortest settings section in Burp. Comparer has exactly **one** option.

## Sync Comparer results view

**Sync Comparer results view** determines whether the panels in **new** comparison results windows will be **synchronized by default**. When enabled, both panels (the left and right compared items) scroll together: you scroll one, the second follows, and the corresponding fragments always stand opposite each other.

## Why this is needed

On short data the difference is visible anyway. But when you compare long HTTP responses and there are several differences in different places, without synchronization the panels quickly "drift apart," and matching up the highlighted pieces becomes painful. This setting simply sets the **default behavior** for each new comparison window, so that you do not have to enable synchronization manually every time.

> In the results window itself, synchronization can still be toggled with the **Sync views** button; the setting only determines the startup state.

```
Settings -> Tools -> Comparer -> Sync Comparer results view
(enable it if you often compare long responses)
```

entries.title: Burp's browser settings - browser data, sandbox, GPU
entries.body:
# Burp's browser settings

Burp has a built-in browser (Chromium), already configured to work through Burp's proxy with its certificate - this is the simplest way to run traffic into Burp without manually configuring an external browser. It does not have many settings, and they are of different storage levels.

## Browser data

* **Store browser data** - whether to store browser data (settings, history) in a special browser data folder. If you uncheck it, the data is not saved between launches - convenient for clean, isolated sessions.
* **Clear all** - a button that wipes accumulated browser data (history, settings).

> These are **user** settings (user-level): they apply to the entire Burp installation on the machine.

## Browser running

* **Allow Burp's browser to run without a sandbox** - allow the browser to launch **without a sandbox**. Sometimes without this the browser does not start (especially as root or in certain environments/containers), but it **reduces isolation**: when visiting a potentially malicious site without a sandbox, the risk of compromising your machine is higher. Enable it only when there is no other way, and you understand the risk.
* **Use the GPU** - allow the browser to use the GPU. Warning: if there is no hardware GPU (for example, in a VM), an attempt to access a nonexistent GPU may lead to browser crashes - then leave it disabled.

> These are **project** settings (project-level): they apply only to the current project. Nearby, in the browser's **Help** menu, there is a **Health check for Burp's browser** tool - a check of whether the browser is capable of running browser-powered scans (including with the sandbox).

```
If Burp's browser won't start:
1. Help -> run Health check for Burp's browser
2. if necessary, enable Allow Burp's browser to run without a sandbox
   (understanding the reduced isolation)
3. in a VM without a GPU -> keep Use the GPU OFF
```

entries.title: Project settings - overview of the project sections
entries.body:
# Project settings (Project)

The **Project** section in the **Settings** dialog gathers the parameters that relate **only to the current project** and are stored **inside the project file**. The page itself is a table-of-contents pointer to five subsections. Unlike user settings, these are not automatically carried over to other projects: they describe a specific test.

## The five sections

* **Scope** - the target scope: which hosts and URLs are included in the testing of this project, and what to do with out-of-scope requests.
* **Collaborator** - exactly how this project works with **Burp Collaborator** (out-of-band tests): the default public server, a private server, or disable it entirely.
* **Tasks** - managing the project's tasks: resource pools (load limits), auto-start/pause of new tasks, the task engine schedule.
* **Automatic backup** - automatic backup saving of the project file by interval (for disk projects).
* **Logging** - which Burp tools write HTTP requests and responses to the project's log files.

## How to use it

```
Settings -> Project -> <section>
```

> Remember: everything in this section sits in the project file. If a parameter is "not visible" in another project - that is normal, it is a project setting. Some of them (for example, Collaborator) can override the user default through **Override options for this project only**.

entries.title: Scope settings (project) - the target scope and drop out-of-scope
entries.body:
# The project's target scope (Scope)

This settings page covers two questions: **what counts as scope** for the current project and **what to do with out-of-scope requests**.

## Target scope - what is in scope

This is where you define which hosts and URLs are **in scope** for the current project. The rules are set by two lists:

* **Include in scope** - what to include (the **Add** button: you can add by a specific URL/prefix or by a regular expression; the protocol, host or IP range, port, file/directory path are set).
* **Exclude from scope** - what to exclude from what is included (for example, throw out `/logout` or a section that must not be touched).

An item is considered **in scope** if it matches one of the Include rules and at the same time does not match any Exclude rule. The site map, Proxy history and scan-coverage filters rely on this scope. The detailed rule-placement wizard lives on a separate page, "Set the target scope" - but it is all enabled exactly from here.

> The fastest way to set the scope in practice: in **Target > Site map** right-click a host node -> **Add to scope**, and then fine-tune the rules here.

## Out-of-scope request handling - what to do with out-of-scope requests

* **Drop all out-of-scope requests** - do not let a single out-of-scope request out, **even if the browser explicitly requested it**. A harsh but reliable way to guarantee that Burp does not knock on an extraneous host (critical in bug bounty - not to go beyond what is allowed).

Which scope to drop based on is chosen with a toggle:

* **Use suite scope** - use the suite-wide target scope;
* **Use custom scope** - set a **separate** scope specifically for the drop rule in this project (includes its own **Include in scope / Exclude from scope** lists for the drop).

```
A safe configuration for bug bounty:
1. Include in scope: only the hosts/domains allowed by the program
2. Exclude from scope: what is forbidden to touch (logout, prod payments, etc.)
3. Drop all out-of-scope requests: ON
4. Use suite scope (or Use custom scope for a narrower drop rule)
```

entries.title: Collaborator settings (project) - public, private server, polling
entries.body:
# Collaborator settings (project)

**Burp Collaborator** is an external service for **out-of-band** vulnerability detection: Burp generates unique domains, and if the vulnerable application "reaches out" to such a domain (DNS, HTTP, SMTP), you will see it - this is how blind SSRF, blind XXE, blind injections, delayed interactions are caught. This page sets **which server** the current project works with and **how**.

## Choosing the server

* **Use the default Collaborator server** - PortSwigger's public server. Simple and configuration-free, but PortSwigger **does not guarantee** availability and performance: when the public service is down, the Collaborator features will stop working.
* **Don't use Burp Collaborator** - completely disable all Collaborator capabilities in the project.
* **Use a private Collaborator server** - connect to **your own** self-hosted Collaborator server instead of the public one (needed for private networks and when interactions must not be released outward to PortSwigger).

## Configuring the private server

When choosing private mode, you specify:

* **Server location** - the domain name or IP of your server (for DNS interactions you specifically need a domain).
* **Polling location** (optional) - where the server answers polling requests: a host/IP and, if necessary, a port, for example `10.20.30.40:8008`.

## Miscellaneous

* **Poll over unencrypted HTTP** - by default Burp polls the server over HTTPS with TLS verification; this flag allows polling over **unencrypted HTTP**, when network restrictions do not let you go directly over HTTPS.
* **Run health check** - diagnostics: check that the configured server is reachable and responds correctly over the various network services.

## Storage level

The **Override options for this project only** toggle determines whether the settings are taken from the user level (common to all installations) or set individually for this project. Convenient: the global default is the public server, but in a sensitive project you enable a private one, without changing the global settings.

```
When to use what:
- quick bug bounty, outward is allowed     -> Use the default Collaborator server
- private network / outward forbidden       -> Use a private Collaborator server
- OOB interactions forbidden entirely        -> Don't use Burp Collaborator
Not working -> Run health check (verify the server is reachable)
```

entries.title: Tasks settings (project) - resource pools, auto-start, schedule
entries.body:
# Tasks settings

This section manages the project's **background tasks** (they are used, in particular, by the **Scanner** and **Intruder**): how much load to put on the target, whether to start tasks immediately and when the task engine should run.

## Resource pools

A resource pool is a "load budget" that you give to tasks, so as not to take down the target and not to run into its defenses. You create a pool and configure:

* **Maximum concurrent requests** - the limit of simultaneously sent requests. The main aggressiveness regulator: the lower the number, the gentler on the server.
* **Delay between requests** (ms) - the pause between requests: fixed, with a random spread, or with incremental growth. Helps to get around rate-limiting and look less like a "robot."
* **Automatic throttling** - automatically introduce a delay when receiving certain response codes: **429** (Too many requests), **503** (Service unavailable) and/or your own codes as a list (CSV). A reaction to a "slow down" from the server, so as not to get a ban.

Pools can be **exported/imported as JSON** (the settings icon), reusing a successful load configuration between projects.

## New task auto-start - the behavior of new tasks

* **Start tasks** - new tasks **start automatically** when created.
* **Pause tasks** - new tasks are created **paused** (you start them manually when ready).

## Schedule tasks - the task engine schedule

Automatically **pause and resume** the task execution engine at a given time. For a rule you specify:

* the rule type (pause/resume the engine);
* the start time and date;
* an optional recurrence (minutes, hours, days).

Actions: **Add / Edit / Remove** for the rules. A classic is to run heavy scans at night, and pause for the day.

> All settings in the section are **project**-level, they apply only to the current project.

```
A safe load on a fragile target:
- Maximum concurrent requests: lower it (for example, 1-5)
- Delay between requests: add a fixed/random pause
- Automatic throttling: react to 429 and 503
- Schedule: heavy stuff at night (pause/resume the engine)
```

entries.title: Automatic backup settings (project) - auto-backup of the project file
entries.body:
# Automatic backup

This section sets how Burp **periodically saves** the project file by itself, so that on a crash/hang you do not lose hours of work.

## The main setting

* **Automatically back up the project every [X] minutes** - enable auto-backup and set the interval in minutes. The section's main parameter: from then on Burp silently saves the project at this interval.

## Backup behavior

Three additional controls over how the backup proceeds:

* **in-scope items only** - put only what is in scope into the backup (more compact, faster on noisy targets);
* **show a progress dialog** during the backup;
* **delete the backup file on exit** from Burp (cleanup on shutdown), if it is not needed after closing.

## Important limitations

* Auto-backup works **only for disk projects**. For **temporary** projects the option is **unavailable (grayed out)** - a temporary project lives in memory anyway and has no permanent file to back up.
* These are **user** settings (user-level): they apply to the entire Burp installation on the machine.

> The practical takeaway: do serious work in a **disk** project and enable auto-backup - otherwise you will lose a temporary project on the very first crash.

```
Reasonable:
- the project is a disk one (not temporary)
- Automatically back up every: 10-15 minutes
- in-scope only, if the target is noisy
```

entries.title: Logging settings (project) - which tools write to the log files
entries.body:
# Logging

This section determines which Burp tools may **append HTTP requests and responses to** the project's **log files**. These are separate log files on disk - not to be confused with **Proxy history** (the history in the project's memory) and not to be confused with the **Logger** tool.

## Which tools can be logged

Writing from each tool is enabled selectively:

* **Proxy**
* **Scanner**
* **Repeater**
* **Intruder**
* **Sequencer**
* **Extender** (extensions)

There is also an **All tools** option - enable logging across the whole set at once.

## What exactly to write

For each selected tool you choose the content type:

* requests only;
* responses only;
* both requests and responses.

## Level and application

These are **project** settings - they apply only to the current project. File logs are useful for post-analysis, reporting and the evidence base (what was sent and when). Note: writing everything across all tools is a lot of data to disk; usually logging is enabled selectively for a specific task.

> Do not confuse three things: **Proxy history** (the project's memory) -> **Logging** (this section, appending to files on disk) -> the **Logger** tool (live viewing and searching across all traffic).

```
Typically:
- need a file log for a report -> enable the necessary tools
- writes requests and responses for completeness of evidence
- do not enable All tools without need (data volume)
```
